key_insight: In cold-rolled Fe-3%Si, geometrically necessary boundaries (GNBs) are the main structures that store and distribute long-range residual elastic strain, while IDB cell structures form inside these strain-homogeneous regions.
engineering_relevance: This clarifies how deformation substructures partition strain in ferritic steels, providing directly useful input for recovery and recrystallization models in electrical steels and similar alloys.
method: Dark-field X-ray microscopy (DFXM) was used to map intragranular misorientation and residual elastic strain simultaneously.
The measurements were performed in three dimensions within a grain of 50% cold-rolled Fe-3%Si.
GNBs and incidental dislocation boundary (IDB) cell structures were segmented and correlated with elastic strain fields.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports 3D, bulk-sensitive DFXM data showing that GNBs separate subdomains with distinct mean d-spacing, while IDB cells within them exhibit similar elastic strain levels.
watch_item: The conclusions are based on a single-grain, non-destructive 3D measurement, so broader generalization to other grains, textures, or alloys should be validated.
paper_title: Geometrically necessary boundaries accommodate the residual elastic strain in cold-rolled Fe-3%Si
